title: "MAT 142: Exponential Functions"
author: Dr. Gilbert
format: 
  revealjs:
    smaller: true
date: today
date-format: long
theme: serif
incremental: true
---

```{r global-options, include=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)

theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 16))
```

```{css}
code.sourceCode {
  font-size: 1.3em;
  /* or try font-size: xx-large; */
}

a {
  color: purple;
}

a:link {
  color: purple;
}

a:visited {
  color: purple;
}
```

## Reminders

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

Last class we reviewed laws of exponents and logarithms, and practiced solving exponential and logarithmic equations. Try the following warm-up problems.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 1:** Solve $\displaystyle{2^{2x+3} = 2^{x+9}}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Hint:* Since the bases are equal, the exponents must be equal.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 2:** Solve $\displaystyle{\frac{1}{5}\left(8^{5-6x}\right) + 9 = 20}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Hint:* Isolate the exponential first, then take the natural logarithm of both sides.

:::

</div>

## Objectives

:::{.fragment}

Today we focus on *exponential functions*.

:::

:::{.fragment}

These are functions that model growth and decay across science, finance, medicine, and data analysis. 

:::

:::{.fragment}

From population growth to radioactive decay to compound interest, exponential functions describe some of the most important patterns in the natural and social world.

:::

:::{.fragment}

After today's class meeting, you should be able to:

:::

+ Identify and sketch the graph of an exponential book function $f\left(x\right) = a^x$.
+ Describe the domain, range, asymptote, and end behavior of an exponential function.
+ Apply transformations to sketch graphs of exponential functions.
+ Find the inverse of an exponential function.
+ Apply exponential functions to a variety of applied contexts.

## The Exponential Book Function

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Definition:** The *exponential book function* is $f\left(x\right) = a^x$, where $a > 0$ and $a \neq 1$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

We require $a > 0$ and $a \neq 1$ to avoid degenerate cases:

:::

+ $a = 1$: gives the constant function $f\left(x\right) = 1$ — not interesting as an exponential.
+ $a \leq 0$: $a^x$ is not real-valued for every $x$ (for example, $\displaystyle{(-4)^{1/2}}$ is undefined in $\mathbb{R}$).

:::{.fragment}

The behavior of the function $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = a^x}$ depends on which side of $1$ the base falls:

:::

+ $a > 1$: the function is **increasing** — larger exponents produce larger outputs.
+ $0 < a < 1$: the function is **decreasing** — larger exponents produce smaller outputs.

:::{.fragment}

There is no memorization required here. It follows directly from what exponentiation does.

:::

:::{.fragment}

For example, $\displaystyle{2^3 = 8}$ while $\displaystyle{\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{16}}$.

:::

</div>

## Graphs of the Exponential Book Function

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| fig.height: 4.5

x_vals <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 500)

p1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = 2^x_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 0, y = 1), size = 3, color = "black") +
  annotate("segment", x = -4, xend = 4, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -0.5, yend = 10,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-4, 4), ylim = c(-0.5, 10)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-4, 4, by = 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10, by = 2)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = a^x$, $a > 1$ (shown: $a = 2$)")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())

p2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = (1/2)^x_vals), color = "darkred", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 0, y = 1), size = 3, color = "black") +
  annotate("segment", x = -4, xend = 4, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -0.5, yend = 10,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-4, 4), ylim = c(-0.5, 10)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-4, 4, by = 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10, by = 2)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = a^x$, $0 < a < 1$ (shown: $a = 1/2$)")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())

p1 + p2
```

## Geography of the Exponential Function

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

Both graphs share the following features regardless of base:

:::

:::{.fragment}

| Feature | Value |
|:--|:--|
| **Domain** | $\left(-\infty, \infty\right)$ — all real numbers |
| **Range** | $\left(0, \infty\right)$ — always positive |
| **$y$-intercept** | $\left(0, 1\right)$ — since $a^0 = 1$ for any premissible $a$ |
| **Horizontal asymptote** | $y = 0$ |

:::

:::{.fragment}

**End behavior depends on the base:**

:::

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

If $a > 1$:

\begin{align} \lim_{x \to -\infty} a^x &= 0\\
\lim_{x \to \infty} a^x &= \infty
\end{align}

:::

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

If $0 < a < 1$:

\begin{align} \lim_{x \to -\infty} a^x &= \infty\\
\lim_{x \to \infty} a^x &= 0
\end{align}

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## The Natural Base $e$

:::{.fragment}

One of the most interesting and widely useful bases in mathematics is *Euler's Number*, $e \approx 2.71828\ldots$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

It arises naturally from as the value of the limit $\displaystyle{\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)^n}$, which we will encounter again shortly in an application to compound interest.

:::

:::{.fragment}

The function $f\left(x\right) = e^x$ is called the *natural exponential function* and is the default choice for most mathematical and scientific applications.

:::

:::{.fragment}

Its inverse is the *natural logarithm* $\ln\left(x\right) = \log_e\left(x\right)$, which is why $\ln$ appeared so naturally when solving exponential equations last class.

:::

## Transformations of Exponential Functions

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

Recall the general transformation framework $g\left(x\right) = A \cdot f\left(B\left(x - H\right)\right) + K$, which 

+ shifts the graph of $f$ right by $H$, 
+ compresses/stretches horizontally by $B$, 
+ stretches vertically by $\left|A\right|$ (reflecting over the $x$-axis if $A < 0$), and 
+ shifts up by $K$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

For exponential functions, the key effects of transformations $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = A\left(a^{B\left(x - H\right)}\right) + K}$ are:

:::

+ The **horizontal asymptote** shifts from $y = 0$ to $y = K$.
+ The **range** shifts from $\left(0, \infty\right)$ to $\left(K, \infty\right)$ or $\left(-\infty, K\right)$ if $A < 0$.
+ The **$y$-intercept** changes — evaluate $f\left(0\right)$ to find it.
+ The **end behavior** changes accordingly. Evaluate the usual limits or reason via the transformation steps.

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$.

:::

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}


:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

:::{.fragment}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

:::{.fragment}

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4
\end{align}

:::

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}


:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

:::{.fragment}

**Horizontal Asymptote:**

:::

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}


:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}


:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:**

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** The book function has no $x$-intercept, but due to the flipping and vertical shift, the function $f\left(x\right)$ will have one.

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} f\left(x\right) &= 0
\end{align}

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** The book function has no $x$-intercept, but due to the flipping and vertical shift, the function $f\left(x\right)$ will have one.

\begin{align} f\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} + 4 &= 0
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** The book function has no $x$-intercept, but due to the flipping and vertical shift, the function $f\left(x\right)$ will have one.

\begin{align} f\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} + 4 &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} &= -4
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** The book function has no $x$-intercept, but due to the flipping and vertical shift, the function $f\left(x\right)$ will have one.

\begin{align} f\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} + 4 &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} &= -4\\
\implies e^{x + 3} &= 2
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** The book function has no $x$-intercept, but due to the flipping and vertical shift, the function $f\left(x\right)$ will have one.

\begin{align} f\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} + 4 &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} &= -4\\
\implies e^{x + 3} &= 2\\
\implies \ln\left(e^{x + 3}\right) &= \ln\left(2\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** The book function has no $x$-intercept, but due to the flipping and vertical shift, the function $f\left(x\right)$ will have one.

\begin{align} f\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} + 4 &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} &= -4\\
\implies e^{x + 3} &= 2\\
\implies \ln\left(e^{x + 3}\right) &= \ln\left(2\right)\\
\implies \left(x + 3\right)\ln\left(e\right) &= \ln\left(2\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** The book function has no $x$-intercept, but due to the flipping and vertical shift, the function $f\left(x\right)$ will have one.

\begin{align} f\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} + 4 &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} &= -4\\
\implies e^{x + 3} &= 2\\
\implies \ln\left(e^{x + 3}\right) &= \ln\left(2\right)\\
\implies \left(x + 3\right)\ln\left(e\right) &= \ln\left(2\right)\\
\implies x + 3 &= \ln\left(2\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** The book function has no $x$-intercept, but due to the flipping and vertical shift, the function $f\left(x\right)$ will have one.

\begin{align} f\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} + 4 &= 0\\
\implies -2e^{x + 3} &= -4\\
\implies e^{x + 3} &= 2\\
\implies \ln\left(e^{x + 3}\right) &= \ln\left(2\right)\\
\implies \left(x + 3\right)\ln\left(e\right) &= \ln\left(2\right)\\
\implies x + 3 &= \ln\left(2\right)\\
\implies x &= \ln\left(2\right) - 3
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(\ln\left(2\right) - 3, 0\right)}$

:::{.fragment}

**End Behavior:**

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} \lim_{x\to -\infty}{-2e^{x + 3} + 4}
\end{align}

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(\ln\left(2\right) - 3, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:**

\begin{align} \lim_{x\to -\infty}{-2e^{x + 3} + 4} &= -2\left(0\right) + 4
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(\ln\left(2\right) - 3, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:**

\begin{align} \lim_{x\to -\infty}{-2e^{x + 3} + 4} &= -2\left(0\right) + 4\\
&= 4
\end{align}

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} \lim_{x\to \infty}{-2e^{x + 3} + 4}
\end{align}

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(\ln\left(2\right) - 3, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:**

\begin{align} \lim_{x\to -\infty}{-2e^{x + 3} + 4} &= -2\left(0\right) + 4\\
&= 4
\end{align}

\begin{align} \lim_{x\to \infty}{-2e^{x + 3} + 4} &= -\infty
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(\ln\left(2\right) - 3, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:** $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to -\infty}{f\left(x\right)} = 4}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{f\left(x\right)} = -\infty}$

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(\ln\left(2\right) - 3, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:** $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to -\infty}{f\left(x\right)} = 4}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{f\left(x\right)} = -\infty}$

```{r}
x_vals <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
y_vals <- -2*exp(x_vals + 3) + 4
y_base_vals <- exp(x_vals)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x_vals, y_base_vals), color = "blue", alpha = 0.4) +
  #geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = y_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  #geom_point(aes(x = 0, y = -2*exp(3) + 4), size = 3) +
  #geom_point(aes(x = log(2) - 3, y = 0), size = 3) +
  #geom_hline(yintercept = 4, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40", linewidth = 0.8) +
  #annotate("text", x = -5, y = 6, label = "y = 4", color = "gray40", size = 5) +
  annotate("segment", x = -5, xend = 3, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -40, yend = 20,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-5, 3), ylim = c(-40, 20)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-5, 1, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = -2e^{x+3} + 4$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(\ln\left(2\right) - 3, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:** $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to -\infty}{f\left(x\right)} = 4}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{f\left(x\right)} = -\infty}$

```{r}
x_vals <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
y_vals <- -2*exp(x_vals + 3) + 4
y_base_vals <- exp(x_vals)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x_vals, y_base_vals), color = "blue", alpha = 0.4) +
  #geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = y_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 0, y = -2*exp(3) + 4), size = 3) +
  #geom_point(aes(x = log(2) - 3, y = 0), size = 3) +
  #geom_hline(yintercept = 4, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40", linewidth = 0.8) +
  #annotate("text", x = -5, y = 6, label = "y = 4", color = "gray40", size = 5) +
  annotate("segment", x = -5, xend = 3, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -40, yend = 20,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-5, 3), ylim = c(-40, 20)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-5, 1, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = -2e^{x+3} + 4$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(\ln\left(2\right) - 3, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:** $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to -\infty}{f\left(x\right)} = 4}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{f\left(x\right)} = -\infty}$

```{r}
x_vals <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
y_vals <- -2*exp(x_vals + 3) + 4
y_base_vals <- exp(x_vals)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x_vals, y_base_vals), color = "blue", alpha = 0.4) +
  #geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = y_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 0, y = -2*exp(3) + 4), size = 3) +
  #geom_point(aes(x = log(2) - 3, y = 0), size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 4, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray30", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = -5, y = 6, label = "y = 4", color = "gray30", size = 5) +
  annotate("segment", x = -5, xend = 3, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -40, yend = 20,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-5, 3), ylim = c(-40, 20)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-5, 1, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = -2e^{x+3} + 4$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(\ln\left(2\right) - 3, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:** $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to -\infty}{f\left(x\right)} = 4}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{f\left(x\right)} = -\infty}$

```{r}
x_vals <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
y_vals <- -2*exp(x_vals + 3) + 4
y_base_vals <- exp(x_vals)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x_vals, y_base_vals), color = "blue", alpha = 0.4) +
  #geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = y_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 0, y = -2*exp(3) + 4), size = 3) +
  geom_point(aes(x = log(2) - 3, y = 0), size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 4, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray30", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = -5, y = 6, label = "y = 4", color = "gray30", size = 5) +
  annotate("segment", x = -5, xend = 3, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -40, yend = 20,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-5, 3), ylim = c(-40, 20)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-5, 1, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = -2e^{x+3} + 4$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* The base function is $e^x$. Since $e > 1$, the base function exhibits *exponential growth* -- it is an *increasing* function.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

The base function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *left* by $3$
+ *flipped* over the $x$-axis
+ *stretched* by a factor of $2$
+ shifted *upward* by $4$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $f\left(0\right)$

\begin{align} f\left(0\right) &= -2e^{0 + 3} + 4\\
&= -2e^3 + 4
\end{align}

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Is moved from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$ because of the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(\ln\left(2\right) - 3, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:** $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to -\infty}{f\left(x\right)} = 4}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{f\left(x\right)} = -\infty}$

```{r}
x_vals <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
y_vals <- -2*exp(x_vals + 3) + 4
y_base_vals <- exp(x_vals)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x_vals, y_base_vals), color = "blue", alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = y_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 0, y = -2*exp(3) + 4), size = 3) +
  geom_point(aes(x = log(2) - 3, y = 0), size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 4, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray30", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = -5, y = 6, label = "y = 4", color = "gray30", size = 5) +
  annotate("segment", x = -5, xend = 3, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -40, yend = 20,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-5, 3), ylim = c(-40, 20)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-5, 1, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$f(x) = -2e^{x+3} + 4$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x}
\end{align}

:::

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x}\\ 
&= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-\left(2x - 10\right)} - 1
\end{align}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x}\\ 
&= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-\left(2x - 10\right)} - 1\\
&= 8\left(2^{2x - 10}\right) - 1
\end{align}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x}\\ 
&= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-\left(2x - 10\right)} - 1\\
&= 8\left(2^{2x - 10}\right) - 1\\
&= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::{.fragment}

**$y$-intercept:** 

:::

:::

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $g\left(0\right)$

:::{.fragment}

$\displaystyle{g\left(0\right)}$

:::

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $g\left(0\right)$

$\displaystyle{g\left(0\right)= 8\left(2^{2\left(0 - 5\right)}\right) - 1}$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** We'll evaluate $g\left(0\right)$

$\displaystyle{g\left(0\right)= 8\left(2^{2\left(0 - 5\right)}\right) - 1 = 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1}$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

:::{.fragment}

**Horizontal Asymptote:**

:::

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** Shifted from $y = 0$ to $y = -1$ due to the vertical shift.

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

:::{.fragment}

**$x$-intercept:** Again, due to the vertical shift, the function will have an $x$-intercept.

:::

:::{.fragment}

We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 0
\end{align}

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** Again, due to the vertical shift, the function will have an $x$-intercept.

We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1 &= 0
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** Again, due to the vertical shift, the function will have an $x$-intercept.

We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1 &= 0\\
\implies 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) &= 1
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** Again, due to the vertical shift, the function will have an $x$-intercept.

We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1 &= 0\\
\implies 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) &= 1\\
\implies 2^{2\left(x - 5\right)} &= \frac{1}{8}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** Again, due to the vertical shift, the function will have an $x$-intercept.

We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1 &= 0\\
\implies 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) &= 1\\
\implies 2^{2\left(x - 5\right)} &= \frac{1}{8}\\
\implies \ln\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** Again, due to the vertical shift, the function will have an $x$-intercept.

We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies \ln\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** Again, due to the vertical shift, the function will have an $x$-intercept.

We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies \ln\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)\\
\implies 2\left(x - 5\right)\ln\left(2\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** Again, due to the vertical shift, the function will have an $x$-intercept.

We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies \ln\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)\\
\implies 2\left(x - 5\right)\ln\left(2\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)\\
\implies x - 5 &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(2\right)}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** Again, due to the vertical shift, the function will have an $x$-intercept.

We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies x - 5 &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(2\right)}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** Again, due to the vertical shift, the function will have an $x$-intercept.

We'll solve $g\left(x\right) = 0$.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 0\\
\implies x - 5 &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(2\right)}\\
\implies x &= 5 + \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(2\right)}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(5 + \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(2\right)}, 0\right)}$


:::{.fragment}

**End Behavior:** 

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(5 + \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(2\right)}, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:** As an alternative to using formal limits, we can analyse the impact of the transformations on the comparison function.

:::{.fragment}

The comparison function exhibited exponential growth.

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(5 + \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(2\right)}, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:** As an alternative to using formal limits, we can analyse the impact of the transformations on the comparison function.

The comparison function exhibited exponential growth. The transformed function also exhibits exponential growth because there was no vertical flip.

:::{.fragment}

The end behavior to the left approaches the horizontal asymptote at $y = -1$.

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Analysis of Exponential Functions II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Problem:** For $g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

*Solution.* Working with a horizontal flip (a negative $B$ value) is difficult, so we'll rewrite the function first.

\begin{align} g\left(x\right) &= 8\left(2^{2\left(x - 5\right)}\right) - 1
\end{align}

The comparison function is $f\left(x\right) = 2^x$, which is exponential growth since the base exceeds $1$.

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

The comparison function undergoes the following transformations.

:::{.nonincremental}

+ shifted *right* by $5$
+ horizontally *compressed* (sped up) by a factor of $2$
+ vertically *stretched* by a factor of $8$
+ shifted *downward* by $1$

:::

**$y$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(0, 8\left(2^{-10}\right) - 1\right)}$

**Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = -1$

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

**$x$-intercept:** $\displaystyle{\left(5 + \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(2\right)}, 0\right)}$

**End Behavior:** $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to -\infty}{g\left(x\right)} = -1}$ and $\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to \infty}{g\left(x\right)} = \infty}$

:::{.fragment}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| fig.height: 3.25

x_vals <- seq(-1, 6, length.out = 500)
g_vals <- 8*(1/2)^(10 - 2*x_vals) - 1

ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = x_vals, y = g_vals), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray30", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 0, y = 8*(1/2)^10 - 1), size = 3, color = "black") +
  geom_point(aes(x = 5 + (log(1/8)/(2*log(2))), y = 0), size = 3, color = "black") +
  annotate("text", x = 5, y = -0.5, label = "y = -1", color = "gray30", size = 5) +
  annotate("segment", x = -1, xend = 6, y = 0, yend = 0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, xend = 0, y = -1.5, yend = 8,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"), linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-1, 6), ylim = c(-1.5, 8)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-1, 6, by = 1)) +
  labs(title = latex2exp::TeX("$g(x) = 8(1/2)^{10-2x} - 1$")) +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank())
```

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Exponential Function Practice

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Try It! 1:** For $f\left(x\right) = 3^{x - 2} + 1$, identify the $y$-intercept, horizontal asymptote, range, and end behavior. Sketch an approximate graph.

<br/>
<br/>
<br/>
<br/>
<br/>

**Try It! 2:** For $g\left(x\right) = -\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{x+1} + 5$, identify the same features and sketch a graph.

*Before sketching:* The negative leading coefficient will flip the graph. How does that affect the range and end behavior compared to the basic $\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^x$ function?

</div>

## Inverses of Exponential Functions

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

Since exponential functions are one-to-one (they pass both the vertical and horizontal line tests), they are invertible. Their inverses are logarithmic functions — which is exactly the connection we've been building toward.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Strategy (Reminder):** The swap-and-solve method for finding $f^{-1}\left(x\right)$, given a one-to-one function $f\left(x\right)$.

:::

1. Replace $f\left(x\right)$ with $y$.
2. Swap $x$ and $y$.
3. Solve for $y$ — this will require using logarithms to undo the exponential.
4. Replace $y$ with $f^{-1}\left(x\right)$.

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* Replace $f\left(x\right)$ with $y$, swap, then solve.

:::

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} y &= -2e^{x+3} + 4
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$.

*Solution.* Replace $f\left(x\right)$ with $y$, swap, then solve.

\begin{align} y &= -2e^{x+3} + 4\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= -2e^{y + 3} + 4
\end{align}

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$.

*Solution.* Replace $f\left(x\right)$ with $y$, swap, then solve.

\begin{align} y &= -2e^{x+3} + 4\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= -2e^{y + 3} + 4\\
\implies -2e^{y + 3} &= x - 4
\end{align}

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$.

*Solution.* Replace $f\left(x\right)$ with $y$, swap, then solve.

\begin{align} y &= -2e^{x+3} + 4\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= -2e^{y + 3} + 4\\
\implies -2e^{y + 3} &= x - 4\\
\implies e^{y + 3} &= \frac{x - 4}{-2}
\end{align}

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$.

*Solution.* Replace $f\left(x\right)$ with $y$, swap, then solve.

\begin{align} y &= -2e^{x+3} + 4\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= -2e^{y + 3} + 4\\
\implies -2e^{y + 3} &= x - 4\\
\implies e^{y + 3} &= \frac{x - 4}{-2}\\
\implies e^{y + 3} &= \frac{4 - x}{2}
\end{align}

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$.

*Solution.* Replace $f\left(x\right)$ with $y$, swap, then solve.

\begin{align} y &= -2e^{x+3} + 4\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= -2e^{y + 3} + 4\\
\implies -2e^{y + 3} &= x - 4\\
\implies e^{y + 3} &= \frac{x - 4}{-2}\\
\implies e^{y + 3} &= \frac{4 - x}{2}\\
\implies \ln\left(e^{y + 3}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{4 - x}{2}\right)
\end{align}

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$.

*Solution.* Replace $f\left(x\right)$ with $y$, swap, then solve.

\begin{align} y &= -2e^{x+3} + 4\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= -2e^{y + 3} + 4\\
\implies -2e^{y + 3} &= x - 4\\
\implies e^{y + 3} &= \frac{x - 4}{-2}\\
\implies e^{y + 3} &= \frac{4 - x}{2}\\
\implies \ln\left(e^{y + 3}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{4 - x}{2}\right)\\
\implies \left(y + 3\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{4 - x}{2}\right)
\end{align}

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function I

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 3:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{f\left(x\right) = -2e^{x+3} + 4}$.

*Solution.* Replace $f\left(x\right)$ with $y$, swap, then solve.

\begin{align} y &= -2e^{x+3} + 4\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= -2e^{y + 3} + 4\\
\implies -2e^{y + 3} &= x - 4\\
\implies e^{y + 3} &= \frac{x - 4}{-2}\\
\implies e^{y + 3} &= \frac{4 - x}{2}\\
\implies \ln\left(e^{y + 3}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{4 - x}{2}\right)\\
\implies \left(y + 3\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{4 - x}{2}\right)\\
\implies y &= \ln\left(\frac{4 - x}{2}\right) - 3
\end{align}

:::{.fragment}

So $\boxed{~\displaystyle{f^{-1}\left(x\right) = \ln\left(\frac{4 - x}{2}\right) - 3}~}$.

:::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.*

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

\begin{align} y &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1
\end{align}

:::

::::

:::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1}$.

*Solution.*

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

\begin{align} y &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} - 1
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1}$.

*Solution.*

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

\begin{align} y &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} - 1\\
\implies 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= x + 1
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1}$.

*Solution.*

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

\begin{align} y &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} - 1\\
\implies 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= x + 1\\
\implies \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= \frac{x + 1}{8}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1}$.

*Solution.*

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

\begin{align} y &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} - 1\\
\implies 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= x + 1\\
\implies \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= \frac{x + 1}{8}\\
\implies \ln\left(\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right) 
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1}$.

*Solution.*

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

\begin{align} y &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} - 1\\
\implies 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= x + 1\\
\implies \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= \frac{x + 1}{8}\\
\implies \ln\left(\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)\\
\implies \left(10 - 2y\right)\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1}$.

*Solution.*

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

\begin{align} y &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} - 1\\
\implies 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= x + 1\\
\implies \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= \frac{x + 1}{8}\\
\implies \ln\left(\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)\\
\implies \left(10 - 2y\right)\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)\\
\implies 10 - 2y &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

:::{.fragment}

\begin{align} \implies -2y &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)} - 10
\end{align}

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1}$.

*Solution.*

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

\begin{align} y &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} - 1\\
\implies 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= x + 1\\
\implies \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= \frac{x + 1}{8}\\
\implies \ln\left(\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)\\
\implies \left(10 - 2y\right)\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)\\
\implies 10 - 2y &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

\begin{align} \implies -2y &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)} - 10\\
\implies y &= \frac{\frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)} - 10}{-2}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1}$.

*Solution.*

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

\begin{align} y &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} - 1\\
\implies 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= x + 1\\
\implies \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= \frac{x + 1}{8}\\
\implies \ln\left(\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)\\
\implies \left(10 - 2y\right)\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)\\
\implies 10 - 2y &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

\begin{align} \implies -2y &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)} - 10\\
\implies y &= \frac{\frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)} - 10}{-2}\\
\implies y &= 5 - \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}
\end{align}

:::

::::

</div>

## Finding the Inverse of an Exponential Function II

<div style="font-size:16pt">

**Example 4:** Find the inverse of $\displaystyle{g\left(x\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1}$.

*Solution.*

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="40%"}

\begin{align} y &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10-2x} - 1\\
\stackrel{\text{swap}}{} x &= 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} - 1\\
\implies 8\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= x + 1\\
\implies \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y} &= \frac{x + 1}{8}\\
\implies \ln\left(\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{10 - 2y}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)\\
\implies \left(10 - 2y\right)\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)\\
\implies 10 - 2y &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)}
\end{align}

:::

:::{.column width="60%"}

\begin{align} \implies -2y &= \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)} - 10\\
\implies y &= \frac{\frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{\ln\left(1/2\right)} - 10}{-2}\\
\implies y &= 5 - \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}
\end{align}

So $\boxed{~\displaystyle{f^{-1}\left(x\right) = 5 - \frac{\ln\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}{2\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}}~}$

:::{.fragment}

Using the change of base formula simplifies the function form slightly to $\boxed{~\displaystyle{f^{-1}\left(x\right) = 5 - \frac{1}{2}\log_{1/2}\left(\frac{x + 1}{8}\right)}~}$

:::

:::

::::

</div>

## Inverse Practice

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Try It! 3:** Find the inverse of $f\left(x\right) = 3^{x-2} + 1$ from Try It! 1. State the domain of $f^{-1}$.

<br/>
<br/>
<br/>
<br/>
<br/>
<br/>

**Try It! 4:** Find the inverse of $g\left(x\right) = -\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{x+1} + 5$ from Try It! 2. State the domain of $g^{-1}$.

</div>

## Compound Interest

<div style="font-size:16pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Definition:** If $P$ dollars (the *principal*) earns interest at annual rate $r$, compounded $n$ times per year, then the account value after $t$ years is:

$$A = P\left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right)^{nt}$$

:::

:::{.fragment}
